source_intake_record.raw_question_text: A lens with a focal length of 25 cm has its central portion of width $a = 3$ mm removed, and the remaining two parts are glued together. As shown in Figure, this type of lens is called a biprism lens. A monochromatic point source with a wavelength of 600.0 nm is placed on the left axis of this lens, and an observation screen is placed on the other side. During the observation of the interference fringes, it is found that the fringe spacing $\Delta x$ remains constant when the observation screen is moved along the axis. Find the fringe spacing $\Delta x$ on the screen

image_dependency: question_text explicitly says 'As shown in the figure', indicating the setup diagram is part of the problem statement
the task involves an optical arrangement (biprism lens, source, screen, axis) where spatial geometry is typically conveyed by the figure
multi_step: the problem is not direct recall; it asks for a derived fringe spacing from several given quantities and conditions
question_text combines lens geometry (focal length, removed central width), wave property (wavelength), and an observational condition (fringe spacing remains constant as screen moves)
solving likely requires linking image formation / effective source separation with interference-fringe relations, i.e., at least two reasoning steps
multiple-choice format with numeric options suggests a calculational physics problem rather than a single visual lookup
verifiability: answer_text is a single numeric result with unit: 0.5 mm
choices are provided, so the final answer can be checked against a fixed option set
the physics result is objective and amenable to formula-based verification
registry_passed=true and both question and answer are present

decision_reasons: Core assets are complete: question text, primary image, and answer are all present, and registry_passed is true.
The sample appears highly image-dependent (image_dependency_score 0.95), so it is valuable for the candidate pool.
The problem is reasonably multi-step (multi_step_score 0.74) and highly verifiable (verifiability_score 0.9) with a fixed numeric answer.
The only noted risk is answer_type_mismatch_between_input_and_registry, which is a metadata consistency concern rather than a loss of core problem semantics.
